content:
First write the sum in closed‐form:

\[
S \;=\;\sum_{k=1}^{2010}9^k
\;=\;9\;\frac{9^{2010}-1}{9-1}
\;=\;\frac{9\,(9^{2010}-1)}{8}.
\]

A prime \(p\neq2\) divides \(S\) iff \(p\mid9\,(9^{2010}-1)\), i.e. either \(p=3\) or else
\[
9^{2010}\equiv1\pmod p,
\]
which means the multiplicative order \(d=\operatorname{ord}_p(9)\) divides \(2010\).  We check primes in increasing order:

2)  Since there are \(2010\) odd terms in the sum, \(S\) is even, so \(2\mid S\).

3)  Trivially \(3\mid9^k\) for all \(k\), so \(3\mid S\).

5)  Mod 5, \(9\equiv4\equiv-1\), so
\[
S\equiv\sum_{k=1}^{2010}(-1)^k\equiv1005\cdot1+1005\cdot(-1)=0\pmod5,
\]
so \(5\mid S\).

7)  Mod 7, \(9\equiv2\) and \(\operatorname{ord}_7(2)=3\mid2010\), so \(7\mid S\).

11)  \(\operatorname{ord}_{11}(9)=5\mid2010\), so \(11\mid S\).

13)  \(\operatorname{ord}_{13}(9)=3\mid2010\), so \(13\mid S\).

17)  One checks \(\operatorname{ord}_{17}(9)=8\), but \(8\nmid2010\).  Hence
\[
9^{2010}\not\equiv1\pmod{17},
\]
and so \(17\nmid S\).

Thus the smallest prime failing to divide \(S\) is

\[
\boxed{17}.
\]

\[
S \;=\;\sum_{k=1}^{2010}9^k
\;=\;9\;\frac{9^{2010}-1}{9-1}
\;=\;\frac{9\,(9^{2010}-1)}{8}.

\[
9^{2010}\equiv1\pmod p,

2)  Since there are \(2010\) odd terms in the sum, \(S\) is even, so \(2\mid S\).

3)  Trivially \(3\mid9^k\) for all \(k\), so \(3\mid S\).

5)  Mod 5, \(9\equiv4\equiv-1\), so
\[
S\equiv\sum_{k=1}^{2010}(-1)^k\equiv1005\cdot1+1005\cdot(-1)=0\pmod5,
\]
so \(5\mid S\).

7)  Mod 7, \(9\equiv2\) and \(\operatorname{ord}_7(2)=3\mid2010\), so \(7\mid S\).

11)  \(\operatorname{ord}_{11}(9)=5\mid2010\), so \(11\mid S\).

13)  \(\operatorname{ord}_{13}(9)=3\mid2010\), so \(13\mid S\).

17)  One checks \(\operatorname{ord}_{17}(9)=8\), but \(8\nmid2010\).  Hence
\[
9^{2010}\not\equiv1\pmod{17},
